topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the screen, her breath catching in shallow, rapid bursts. The words, her carefully crafted narrative about overcoming a personal setback, were laid out in stark, unadorned text. It was *her* essay. It was published on the university’s admissions page as a model submission. A cold dread seeped into her bones, making her hands tremble so violently she had to grip the desk edge to steady them. She reread the paragraphs, searching for a misplaced comma, a sign of forgery, anything to prove this was a mistake. But it was perfect. Too perfect. A wave of nausea washed over her, and she squeezed her eyes shut, trying to physically block the image from her vision. When she opened them, the light seemed too harsh, and the silence of her dorm room amplified the frantic drumming in her chest.

[story 2]

Mark felt a sudden, sickening lurch in his stomach as he scrolled through the online portal. There, nestled between hundreds of hopeful submissions, was his personal statement. It was displayed prominently, labeled 'Featured Example.' His knuckles whitened as he gripped his mouse, his jaw tightening until a dull ache radiated across his temples. He clicked on it, and the words swam before his eyes—his vulnerability, his deepest reflections—all polished and presented as an accessible blueprint for future applicants. A dry, rasping sound escaped his throat. He leaned back from the monitor, the cheap plastic chair groaning beneath him, and began pacing the small confines of his room, his movements jerky and uncontrolled, unable to settle his gaze on anything but the glowing text.

[story 3]

“No. No, this isn’t right.” Liam whispered, his voice thin and reedy in the quiet library aisle. He had stumbled upon it while researching essay structure for a different course. His heart hammered against his ribs, a frantic, trapped bird. The essay was undeniably his—the specific anecdotes, the unique phrasing—but it was displayed under a banner that read ‘Illustrative Submission.’ A flush crept up his neck, staining his cheeks crimson. He snatched the printed copy from the nearby stack, his fingers fumbling, and immediately began tearing at the paper, the sound of the ripping sound seeming deafening in the hushed space. He couldn't look up; he just kept shredding the evidence, his breathing shallow and ragged.
